questions.question: A 4-hour agent run reboots halfway through. What does durable execution do that a naive while-True loop does not?
options: Nothing — both restart from scratch

Resume from the latest checkpoint: completed activities replay their logged results instead of re-executing, so tool side effects don't repeat, approvals aren't re-requested, and prior LLM calls aren't re-billed
Switch to a faster model on resume
Email the user an apology
questions.explanation: The naive loop re-runs the three tool calls (real side effects), re-prompts the user, and re-bills forty LLM calls. Durable execution replays from the event log — a decade-old workflow pattern with LLM calls as the new kind of activity.

questions.question: Why do LLM calls fit the workflow "activity" profile so cleanly?
options: They are deterministic and free
They are non-deterministic, expensive, potentially failing, and side-effectful — exactly the activity profile, so wrapping them gives retry, checkpointing, and a replayable trace
They never fail
They run instantly
questions.explanation: Activities are the non-deterministic units that get logged with inputs and outputs; the workflow is the deterministic orchestration that can be replayed. LLM calls drop into the activity slot perfectly.

questions.question: Why must the workflow (orchestration) code be deterministic while activities need not be?
options: Determinism makes it run faster
On recovery the workflow re-runs from the start and replays completed activities' logged results — non-deterministic orchestration would diverge from the recorded event log
Activities are written in a different language
Determinism is a billing requirement
questions.explanation: Same shape as React re-rendering against a virtual DOM or Git rebuilding a tree from commits: determinism in the orchestrator is what makes durability cheap. Activities replay from their logged outputs; the orchestration must reach the same decisions.

questions.question: What is METR's "35-minute degradation," and how should durable execution respond to it?
options: Sessions are capped at 35 minutes by law

Reliability decays beyond ~35 minutes (doubling duration roughly quadruples failure rate) — durability lets runs exceed the reliability profile, so pair it with HITL-on-resume and budget kill switches
Models need a 35-minute warmup
Checkpoints must be 35 minutes apart
questions.explanation: Durable execution doesn't fix the decay; it enables runs longer than the reliability supports — a new way to fail unsafely if mishandled. The safe pattern combines durability with fresh HITL on re-entry and compute caps regardless of wall-clock time.

questions.question: When is durable execution the WRONG choice?
options: For any agent that calls an LLM
For sub-few-minute runs with no human input, strictly read-only retrieval, or tasks needing end-to-end correctness within one context window — overhead exceeds benefit
Whenever PostgreSQL is unavailable
For tasks longer than an hour
questions.explanation: Durability has real overhead. Short, side-effect-free, or single-context-window tasks don't benefit. The human-input-as-first-class-state and replay machinery pay off specifically for long, side-effecting, approval-gated runs.
